text: Science underpins many decisions that policymakers have to make, especially in times of global crisis. For those decisions to be made on the basis of evidence, it is important that the public trusts scientists enough to translate their findings into policy. This trust has come under threat in recent years, however, fueling speculation about a global science trust crisis. Our pre-registered survey of 71,922 people in 68 countries finds that, for the vast majority of countries, a large majority of the public trust scientists, believe that scientists should become more involved in society and policy-making, and endorse a set of institutional arrangements intended to strengthen the relations between science and society. While trust varies between countries and between individuals within countries — reflecting factors such as party affiliation and religiosity — our results show no sign of a global science trust crisis. Even if a minority is untrustful, this is enough to make a difference to the politics of science — as policymakers know only too well. Our results provide scientists and policymakers with a set of new tools with which to think about the relations between science and society.
headline: Fully Human Written
